title: Trustworthy deep learning for cyber-physical systems
abstract: In cyber-physical systems, along with the accuracy of decision-making, interpretability remains one of the important aspects to build user trust and generate domain insights. Therefore, it is important to develop trustworthy deep learning models by interpreting the outcomes from a domain-based understanding. Unfortunately, however, we often find a trade-off between the model complexity, accuracy, and the ability to interpret the outcomes. Accurate predictive modeling in cyber-physical systems coupled with interpretability mechanisms is still a hard technical challenge for the machine learning community. This work focuses on developing trustworthy deep learning models for different cyber-physical systems. Time series prediction has numerous applications in various domains of significant societal impact, such as healthcare, financial markets, agriculture, and climate science, to name a few. We propose the use of temporal attention in the context of time series prediction in which the attention mechanism can soft search and highlight the most important time-steps to predict the output variable. Next, we propose a novel architecture called spatiotemporal attention mechanism (STAM) to simultaneously learn the most important time-steps and variables. We compare the performance of our model on two popular public datasets and a domain-specific dataset. When compared with the baseline models, the results show that STAM can maintain state-of-the-art prediction accuracy while offering the benefit of accurate spatiotemporal interpretability. Such spatial and temporal interpretations can help users better understand the contributions of different features and time-steps for prediction. We validate the learned attention weights from a domain knowledge perspective. Valuable insights can be gained by understanding the predictions from the perspective of 'what' and 'where' highlighted by STAM. We develop models to predict yearly crop yield given the weather information across the crop growing season and to provide valuable insights regarding the important time windows across the crop growing season. After that, we propose a novel multi-level attention model to understand the impact of weather variables on crop yield. In another application based on time series, we develop a deep learning framework to accurately detect the transition to lean blowout of combustion systems. Performance monitoring of cyber-physical systems utilizes video sensing modality to a great extent for enhanced efficiency, safety, and security. In this work, we propose a model called selective autoencoder to detect combustion instability precursors from snippets of hi-speed video in a human-understandable manner. Next, we explore the effectiveness of `attention' as a model interpretability mechanism in a classification framework based on video snippets. We propose a novel 3D deep learning autoencoder model which can utilize domain-relevant information from image-based DMD to detect instability from hi-speed flame video snippets. To establish trust in such models, we attempt to validate the deep learning outcomes from a physics-based understanding. The insights from these models are in accord with the domain knowledge. In many cyber-physical systems, imaging can be an important but expensive or 'difficult to deploy’ sensing modality. Cross-modal reconstruction becomes important in such scenarios to utilize a more feasible sensing modality. In an ablation study with our proposed VSenseNet models, we explore the possibility of virtual sensing with the cross-modal reconstruction of visual features from time series. The results demonstrate that VSenseNet can reconstruct higher dimensional information (image) from lower dimensional information (time series), and detection accuracy can be enhanced using the virtual visual sensing modality. This proposed framework can have broad application interests when certain sensing modalities may be difficult to deploy in the field and can only be tested in laboratory settings. We believe that the work presented here can be a great resource for the domain experts in different sectors who seek trustworthy (not just a black-box) deep learning tools for different cyber-physical systems. Scientists, engineers, medical professionals, farmers, policymakers, and various other users can utilize these models. Therefore, the proposed models can help in data-driven decision-making in various parts of society, well beyond the machine learning community.
